original_abstract: While extensive literature exists regarding the historical development of Pentecostalism in Ghana, the specific pneumatological dimensions of its missional theology remain under-researched. This article addresses this gap by examining the missional role of the Holy Spirit through the lens of Ghanaian Pentecostal belief and practice. Utilizing a trinitarian framework as a point of departure, the study explores how these churches conceptualize the Holy Spirit not merely as a theological abstraction, but as a dynamic agent in the *missio Dei*. The research draws on official doctrinal documents, liturgical expressions, and interviews with church leaders to analyze the importance of the "Spirit phenomenon"—including the baptism and infilling of the Holy Spirit—in equipping the faithful. The findings indicate that for Ghanaian Pentecostals, the Holy Spirit serves a dual missional purpose: convicting the corporate world of sin and empowering individual believers with charismatic gifts for service and witness. The article concludes that within this tradition, the church’s participation in mission is viewed as inextricably linked to the Spirit’s presence; without this divine empowerment, missionary efforts are reduced to mere human activity. Consequently, practices aimed at maintaining a "Spirit-filled" life, such as intensive prayer and scriptural study, are presented as essential missiological strategies for contemporary Christian practice.
humanized_abstract: While there is a plethora of literature concerning the historical development of Pentecostalism in Ghana, relatively little has been written concerning the pneumatological dimensions of the missional theology of the movement. Utilising a trinitarian understanding as a departure point this article examines the missional role of the Holy Spirit within Ghanaian Pentecostal understandings and practices. It engages official documents, liturgy and oral testimony from Ghanaian Pentecostal church leaders in order to understand how the “Spirit phenomenon” such as baptism and being filled with the Holy Spirit functions in empowering believers in order to conduct mission. A Ghanaian Pentecostal understanding affirms that the Holy Spirit as the agent of the *missio Dei* is missionally operative in two ways: convicting the world of its sinfulness and empowering believers with gifts in order that they may serve and witness in the world of humanity. Without the Spirit’s presence the Ghanaian Pentecumenical Pentecostal church members argue that any mission work is nothing more than humanistic endeavour. Thus, in a Ghanaian context, the practise of maintaining a Spirit-filled life through acts of prayer and Bible study amongst other things serve as missiological tools for the mission work undertaken by evangelists and other believers.
